lesson_title: Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.text: A {{c1::converting constructor}} is a constructor that can be called with a {{c2::single argument}}, allowing implicit conversion from that argument type to the class type.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.question: What happens in this code?
cards.code:
class Fraction
{
public:
    Fraction(int num) : m_num{num}, m_den{1} { }
private:
    int m_num, m_den;
};

Fraction f = 5;
cards.answer: Implicit conversion. The int 5 is implicitly converted to Fraction by calling Fraction(5).
cards.explanation: The single-argument constructor acts as a converting constructor, allowing assignment from int to Fraction.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.text:
To prevent implicit conversions, mark the constructor as {{c1::explicit}}:
{{c2::explicit}} ClassName(int value);
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.code:
class String
{
public:
    explicit String(const char* str) { }
};

String s = "hello"; // error!
cards.answer: Cannot use copy initialization with explicit constructor. Must use direct initialization: String s{"hello"};
cards.explanation: The explicit keyword prevents implicit conversions, including copy initialization. Direct initialization is required.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.explanation:
Make constructors explicit to:
  • Prevent unintended implicit conversions
  • Make code more explicit and readable
  • Avoid ambiguity in function overload resolution
  • Prevent subtle bugs from unexpected conversions
Use non-explicit constructors only when implicit conversion is intended and makes sense semantically.
examples: explicit String(int size);  // prevent String s = 10;
Fraction(int n);  // allow Fraction f = 5; if intentional
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.front: What does the explicit keyword do?
cards.back: The explicit keyword prevents a constructor from being used for implicit conversions. The constructor can only be called explicitly with direct initialization, not through copy initialization or implicit type conversion.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.question: What happens when this function is called with an int?
cards.answer: Compiles and works. The int 5 is implicitly converted to Cents{5} to match the parameter type.
cards.explanation: Non-explicit single-argument constructors allow implicit conversion when passing arguments to functions.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.text: Best practice: Make all single-argument constructors {{c1::explicit}} by default, unless you {{c2::specifically intend}} to allow implicit conversions.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.front: What counts as a single-argument constructor for conversion purposes?
cards.back:
• Constructor with exactly one parameter
  • Constructor with multiple parameters where all but the first have default values
Both can act as converting constructors.
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.question: Which initialization works with an explicit constructor?
cards.code:
class Widget
{
public:
    explicit Widget(int x) { }
};

A) Widget w = 5;
B) Widget w{5};
C) Widget w(5);
cards.answer: B and C work (direct initialization). A doesn't work (copy initialization).
cards.explanation: Explicit constructors can only be used with direct initialization (braces or parentheses), not copy initialization (equals).
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.front: Why can implicit conversions with constructors be dangerous?
cards.back:
They can cause unexpected behavior:
  • Accidentally passing wrong type (compile succeeds but logic is wrong)
  • Performance overhead from temporary object creation
  • Ambiguity in overload resolution
  • Code that compiles but doesn't do what you intended
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword

cards.back: Keyword that prevents a constructor from being used for implicit type conversions
cards.source: 14.16 - Converting constructors and the explicit keyword
